elements.sent-2411-010: Diophantine equations involving sums of powers have long captivated mathematicians due to their deep connections with number theory and their potential to reveal insights into the structure of integers.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Erd\"{o s-Moser equation: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{m - 1} i^k = m^k,\end{equation} has remained an enduring challenge, with only one known solution: \(k = 1\), \(m = 3\).}
elements.sent-2411-012: This conjecture was first posed by Paul Erd\"{o s in the 1950s in correspondence with Leo Moser, and later partially verified by Leo Moser, who demonstrated that for any solution with \(k \geq 2\), \(m\) must exceed \(10^{10^{6 }\)~\cite{moser1953diophantine .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Subsequent advancements such as those performed by Gallot, Moree, and Zudilin have significantly improved the lower bound for \(m\), achieving values greater than \(10^{10^{9 }\) by utilizing modern computational techniques and continued fraction methods to explore properties of logarithmic constants~\cite{gallot2011erdHos .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Mathematical approaches to this problem have ranged from elementary inequalities and congruences to advanced methods involving Bernoulli numbers and continued fractions.
elements.sent-2411-015: The use of the von Staudt-Clausen theorem has been pivotal in deriving properties of power sums, and Moree's extension of Moser's work further demonstrated how congruences involving prime powers can provide insights into the nature of potential solutions~\cite{moree2013moser .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These tools have not only helped prove nonexistence results for larger classes of parameters but have also deepened the understanding of why such solutions are rare.
elements.sent-2411-017: Further investigations into generalized versions of the Erd\"{o s-Moser equation, such as those considered by Baoulina and Moree (2015), explored additional constraints and modifications, extending the analysis beyond the original equation.}
elements.sent-2411-018: These generalizations revealed intricate relationships between coefficients, power sums, and integer solutions, emphasizing the broader implications of the Erd\"{o s-Moser conjecture in number theory~\cite{baoulina2016forbidden .}}
elements.sent-2411-019: This paper builds on these previous works by employing exact summation formulas, integral bounds, and detailed error analysis to conclusively prove the uniqueness of the solution \((k,m)=(1,3)\) for all positive integers \(k \geq 2\).
elements.sent-2411-020: Our findings contribute to the broader understanding of Diophantine equations, emphasizing the delicate balance between sums of powers and individual powers in number theory.

elements.subsubsection-2411-001: Approximating \( S(m - 1, k) \) Using the Euler-MacLaurin Formula
elements.sent-2411-023: Let \( m \geq 2 \) and \( k \geq 1 \) be positive integers.
elements.sent-2411-024: The discrete sum \( S(m - 1, k) = \sum_{i=1 ^{m - 1 i^{k \) can be approximated using the Euler-MacLaurin formula~\cite{gould1963maclaurin, knopp1990theory, apostol2013introduction as follows: \begin{equation}S(m - 1, k) \approx S_{R}(m - 1, k) = \int_{1}^{m - 1} x^{k} \, dx + \frac{1}{2}\left[ f(1) + f(m - 1) \right],\end{equation} where \( f(x) = x^{k \).}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}S(m - 1, k) \approx S_{R}(m - 1, k) = \int_{1}^{m - 1} x^{k} \, dx + \frac{1}{2}\left[ f(1) + f(m - 1) \right],\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-025: Evaluating the integral and boundary terms yields: \begin{equation}S_{R}(m - 1, k) = \frac{(m - 1)^{k + 1} - 1}{k + 1} + \frac{1}{2}\left[ 1 + (m - 1)^{k} \right].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-026: The formula states: \begin{equation}\sum_{n=a}^{b} f(n) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx + \frac{f(a) + f(b)}{2} + R,\end{equation} where \( R \), the remainder term, is a sequence involving derivatives of the function f and Bernoulli numbers.
elements.sent-2411-027: For our purposes, we consider the approximation without the remainder term R.
elements.sent-2411-028: This simplification is justified and thoroughly investigated later on.
elements.sent-2411-029: Applying the Euler-MacLaurin formula to \( S(m - 1, k) \) with \( a = 1 \) and \( b = m - 1 \), we have: \begin{equation}S(m - 1, k) \approx \int_{1}^{m - 1} f(x) \, dx + \frac{f(1) + f(m - 1)}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-031: Compute the definite integral of \( f(x) = x^{k \): \begin{equation}\int_{1}^{m - 1} x^{k} \, dx = \left[ \frac{x^{k + 1}}{k + 1} \right]_{x=1}^{x=m - 1} = \frac{(m - 1)^{k + 1} - 1^{k + 1}}{k + 1} = \frac{(m - 1)^{k + 1} - 1}{k + 1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Evaluate \( f(x) \) at the endpoints \( x = 1 \) and \( x = m - 1 \): \begin{equation}f(1) = 1^{k} = 1, \quad f(m - 1) = (m - 1)^{k}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: Thus, the boundary terms are: \begin{equation}\frac{f(1) + f(m - 1)}{2} = \frac{1 + (m - 1)^{k}}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-034: Combining the integral and boundary terms, we obtain the approximation: \begin{equation}S_{R}(m - 1, k) = \frac{(m - 1)^{k + 1} - 1}{k + 1} + \frac{1 + (m - 1)^{k}}{2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-035: This approximation preserves the essential characteristics of the original sum and facilitates further analysis within the framework of real analysis.

elements.paragraph-2411-004: The Euler-MacLaurin Formula with Non-Zero Correction Terms
elements.sent-2411-052: The Euler-MacLaurin formula, including correction terms up to order \( p - 1 \), is: \begin{equation}\sum_{n=a}^{b} f(n) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx + \frac{f(a) + f(b)}{2} + \sum_{r=1}^{p - 1} \frac{B_{2r}}{(2r)!} \left( f^{(2r - 1)}(b) - f^{(2r - 1)}(a) \right) + R_p,\end{equation} where \( B_{2r \) are Bernoulli numbers, and \( R_p \) is the remainder term after including \( p - 1 \) correction terms.}

elements.paragraph-2411-005: Omission of the First Correction Term
elements.sent-2411-054: In our approximation, we have included only the integral and boundary terms, omitting the first correction term \( C \) given by: \begin{equation}C = \frac{B_{2}}{2} \left( f'(b) - f'(a) \right),\end{equation} where \( B_{2 = \dfrac{1 {6 \) is the second Bernoulli number.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: We need to compute \( C \) and analyze the error introduced by its omission.
elements.paragraph-2411-006: Computing the First Correction Term \( C \)
elements.sent-2411-057: Evaluate \( f'(x) \) at \( a = 1 \) and \( b = m - 1 \): \begin{equation}f'(a) = f'(1) = k,\end{equation} \begin{equation}f'(b) = f'(m - 1) = k (m - 1)^{k - 1}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-058: Therefore, the correction term \( C \) is: \begin{equation}C = \frac{B_{2}}{2} \left( f'(b) - f'(a) \right) = \frac{1}{12} \left( k (m - 1)^{k - 1} - k \right) = \frac{k}{12} \left( (m - 1)^{k - 1} - 1 \right).\end{equation}.
elements.paragraph-2411-007: Magnitude of the Correction Term \( C \)
elements.sent-2411-059: We can estimate the magnitude of \( C \): \begin{equation}|C| = \left| \frac{k}{12} \left( (m - 1)^{k - 1} - 1 \right) \right|.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-061: The leading term in our approximation \( S_{\mathbb{R (m - 1, k) \) is: \begin{equation}L = \frac{(m - 1)^{k + 1}}{k + 1}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: We compare \( |C| \) with \( L \) by considering the ratio: \begin{equation}\frac{|C|}{L} = \frac{\dfrac{k}{12} \left( (m - 1)^{k - 1} - 1 \right)}{\dfrac{(m - 1)^{k + 1}}{k + 1}} = \frac{k(k + 1)}{12} \cdot \frac{(m - 1)^{k - 1} - 1}{(m - 1)^{k + 1}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: For \( m \geq 3 \) and \( k \geq 1 \), \( (m - 1) \geq 1 \), and as \( m \) increases, \( (m - 1)^{k + 1 \) grows much faster than \( (m - 1)^{2 \).}}
elements.sent-2411-067: This indicates that \( |C| \) becomes negligible compared to \( L \) for large \( m \).
elements.sent-2411-068: Since \( |C| \) is significantly smaller than the leading term \( L \), especially for larger values of \( m \), omitting the correction term \( C \) introduces an error that is negligible in the context of our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-069: Our primary interest lies in the dominant behavior of \( S_{\mathbb{R (m - 1, k) \) as it relates to \( m^{k \), and the omission of \( C \) does not affect this relationship.}}}

elements.sent-2411-101: We begin by evaluating the sum on the left-hand side of \eqref{eq:case1_equation .}

elements.sent-2411-104: Substituting this into \eqref{eq:case1_equation , we have \begin{equation}\frac{(m - 1)m}{2} = m.\end{equation}.}
